description: The figure is a line graph showing tensile strength (TS, MPa) versus annealing temperature (T, °C) for a series of 8.5Mn-0.2C (wt.%) austenite-ferrite alloys. Data series in the legend correspond to three compositions—6Al, 6Al+XSi, and 7Al—each tested at three hold times (1 min, 136 sec, and 5 min), represented by green diamonds, red squares, and blue triangles, respectively. For every composition and time, TS decreases monotonically as annealing temperature rises from 800°C to 1000°C, reflecting softening during recrystallization. The 6Al+XSi alloy consistently records the highest strength (approximately 800 MPa at 800°C), followed by 7Al (~758 MPa) and base 6Al (~743 MPa), evidencing solid-solution strengthening from added Si and higher Al. Prolonged annealing generally causes minor additional strength loss. Collectively, the data establish that while Al and Si substantially raise the strength of two-phase low-density steels, this advantage is progressively eroded at higher annealing temperatures.
